snappy: Think of it as a guided missile for cancer: an antibody that hunts down tumor cells, fused to a chemo drug that destroys them on contact.
detail: ADCs deliver toxic chemotherapy directly to cancer cells while sparing healthy ones, which means stronger results with fewer side effects than traditional chemo. They're a fast-growing class across breast, bladder, and lung cancers — well-known examples include Kadcyla (trastuzumab emtansine) and Enhertu.

snappy: Cancer that starts in cells lining your organs and making mucus or fluids — the most common form of lung, colon, prostate, and pancreatic cancer.
detail: Most cancers diagnosed in the lung, colon, prostate, and pancreas are adenocarcinomas — meaning they originate in glandular tissue rather than skin-like or supporting cells. The distinction matters because different cancer types respond to different treatments. You'll see the term in pathology reports, oncology trial enrollment criteria, and any commercial discussion of which patient population a new drug is targeting.

snappy: The four-step journey every drug takes through your body — getting in, getting around, getting broken down, and getting out.
detail: ADME is the framework drug developers use to predict how a drug behaves once it enters the body. Each step affects how much drug reaches the target, how long it lasts, and what side effects emerge. You'll hear ADME on biotech earnings calls when companies discuss whether a candidate has good "drug-like properties," and in early-stage trial design where these properties first get measured.

snappy: The follow-up treatment after surgery or radiation, designed to mop up cancer cells you can't see and stop the disease coming back.
detail: Adjuvant therapy — usually chemo, hormone therapy, or immunotherapy — is what oncologists use to lower the chance of recurrence after the visible tumor has been removed. You'll hear it constantly in breast, colon, and lung cancer trial readouts, often framed around the question "did adjuvant treatment improve survival?"

snappy: Any unwanted or harmful effect a patient experiences during a clinical trial — whether or not the drug actually caused it.
detail: Adverse events are tracked exhaustively in trials because regulators need to know what users might experience. The term covers everything from a mild headache to a serious hospitalization — the more severe ones get reclassified as Serious Adverse Events (SAEs). You'll hear AEs come up in trial readouts ("the safety profile was consistent with…") and in FDA review letters that decide whether a drug gets approved.

snappy: A drug that flips a switch to activate a receptor in your body — telling cells to do something, like a thermostat dialing up the heat.
detail: Agonists mimic natural signaling molecules — when one binds to a receptor, the receptor activates as if its natural partner had arrived. Many medications work this way: opioids agonize pain receptors; asthma inhalers like albuterol agonize beta-2 receptors to relax airways. You'll hear the term in pharmacology basics and any discussion of how a new drug actually does its job in the body.

snappy: A key signaling protein that pushes cells to grow and survive — and is often hijacked by cancer to fuel its own growth.
detail: AKT (also called Protein Kinase B) sits at the center of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, one of the most commonly disrupted signaling networks in cancer. When AKT is overactive, cells refuse to die and keep multiplying — so drugs that block it have been a long-running area of cancer research. You'll hear AKT in oncology trial designs, pipeline discussions, and commercial pitches involving "pathway inhibitors."

snappy: A gene mutation that drives about 5% of lung cancers — and the target of some of oncology's biggest precision-medicine wins.
detail: ALK gene fusions cause uncontrolled cell growth in a subset of non-small-cell lung cancer patients. The story is a precision-medicine showcase: identify the mutation, prescribe a targeted drug like Xalkori (crizotinib) or Alecensa (alectinib), and patients live dramatically longer than they would on chemo. You'll hear ALK in lung cancer trial readouts and at ASCO whenever new generations of inhibitors are presented.

snappy: A fast-growing blood cancer that starts in immature lymphocytes — the most common cancer in children, though it also affects adults.
detail: ALL develops when lymphoid cells in the bone marrow turn cancerous and crowd out healthy blood cells. Treatment is intensive — combination chemo, sometimes a bone marrow transplant, increasingly CAR-T cell therapy in relapsed cases. You'll hear ALL discussed in pediatric oncology contexts and in any commercial story around CAR-T (Kymriah was approved for pediatric and young-adult ALL in 2017).

snappy: A fast-moving blood cancer that starts in the bone marrow and demands urgent treatment — primarily affects older adults but can hit at any age.
detail: AML develops when myeloid cells — the precursors to red cells, platelets, and most white cells — become cancerous and overrun the bone marrow. Treatment is aggressive (high-dose chemo, often bone marrow transplant) and increasingly genotype-driven, with FLT3 and IDH inhibitors targeting specific mutations. You'll hear AML in oncology pipeline discussions, ASCO/ASH conference coverage, and around companies developing targeted leukemia therapies.

snappy: The process where the body builds new blood vessels — which tumors hijack to feed their own growth.
detail: Tumors trick surrounding tissue into growing new blood vessels that supply oxygen and nutrients, letting them expand beyond what local blood supply can sustain. Drugs that block this process — including Avastin (bevacizumab) — are a major class in oncology. You'll hear angiogenesis come up in solid-tumor trial designs and in commercial discussions of how "anti-angiogenics" fit alongside immunotherapy and chemo.

snappy: A drug that blocks a receptor — like sliding tape over a light switch so it can't be turned on.
detail: Antagonists work by occupying a receptor site without activating it, preventing the body's natural signaling molecule from binding. Beta blockers like propranolol are antagonists at beta-adrenergic receptors, slowing the heart. Antihistamines block histamine receptors. You'll hear the term in pharmacology basics, in any drug-development discussion of mechanism, and on earnings calls when companies position their drug against a competitor's.

snappy: A tumor-suppressor gene whose job is to keep cells from dividing out of control — and its breakdown is the first step toward most colorectal cancers.
detail: APC mutations are among the earliest genetic changes in colorectal cancer development — they're found in roughly 80% of sporadic cases and in nearly all patients with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP), an inherited cancer syndrome. You'll hear APC in colorectal cancer screening discussions, hereditary-cancer-syndrome contexts, and in any pipeline conversation involving Wnt-pathway-targeted therapies.

snappy: The actual chemical inside a pill or injection that does the work — everything else (fillers, coatings, capsule) is just delivery.
detail: An API is what's pharmacologically active in a finished drug; the inactive components — called excipients — give it shape, stability, and a delivery mechanism. APIs are typically manufactured at specialized facilities, often in India or China, before being formulated into the final product. You'll hear API in supply-chain discussions (especially around shortages), at drug-pricing debates, and on earnings calls when companies discuss vertical integration.

snappy: Programmed cell death — the body's built-in self-destruct mechanism for damaged or unwanted cells. Cancer cells routinely figure out how to switch it off.
detail: Apoptosis is how the body removes cells that are no longer needed or are too damaged to function — about a million cells per second, in normal physiology. Cancer cells acquire mutations that disable apoptosis, allowing them to keep dividing instead of self-destructing. You'll hear apoptosis come up in cancer-mechanism discussions and in any drug-development pitch around restoring cell death (e.g., BH3 mimetics, BCL-2 inhibitors).

snappy: A laboratory test that measures something — usually whether a drug works, how much of it is present, or what it does to cells.
detail: Assays are the workhorses of drug development: high-throughput screens to find compounds that hit a target, ELISAs to detect proteins in blood, cell-based assays to measure tumor killing. Diagnostic companies sell assays as products in their own right. You'll hear the term across early-stage research papers, biotech investor decks describing platforms, and any FDA discussion of analytical validation.

snappy: A brain tumor that arises from star-shaped support cells called astrocytes — the family includes glioblastoma, the most aggressive.
detail: Astrocytomas range from slow-growing low-grade tumors to glioblastoma multiforme — one of the deadliest cancers, with median survival measured in months despite surgery, radiation, and chemo. The category is graded I to IV by aggressiveness. You'll hear astrocytoma in neuro-oncology contexts, brain-cancer trial readouts, and around clinical-trial sponsors developing therapies for the underserved glioblastoma population.

snappy: A gene that helps cells fix damaged DNA — when it's broken, cells accumulate mutations and cancer risk goes up.
detail: ATM is a key DNA-damage response gene; people who inherit mutations in both copies develop ataxia telangiectasia, a rare disorder with sharply elevated cancer risk. ATM mutations also turn up in breast, prostate, and pancreatic cancers, where they may flag tumors that respond to PARP inhibitors. You'll hear ATM in precision-oncology trial designs and any commercial discussion of biomarker-driven drug selection.

snappy: When the body's immune system mistakes its own healthy cells for invaders and attacks them — sometimes triggered by cancer immunotherapy.
detail: Autoimmune diseases — rheumatoid arthritis, lupus, type 1 diabetes, multiple sclerosis — affect roughly 5% of the population and are a major commercial market for biologic drugs like Humira (adalimumab) and Stelara (ustekinumab). Cancer immunotherapy can also trigger autoimmune-like side effects called immune-related adverse events. You'll hear autoimmune in immunology pipeline discussions, biologic earnings calls, and immunotherapy trial readouts.

snappy: The most common skin cancer — slow-growing, rarely spreads to other organs, but can quietly cause real damage to the skin and underlying tissue if left alone.
detail: BCC arises from the deepest layer of the skin's outer surface, usually on sun-exposed areas like the face, ears, and neck. Treatment is typically surgical removal, with high cure rates. For advanced or rare metastatic cases, hedgehog-pathway inhibitors like Erivedge (vismodegib) are options. You'll hear BCC in dermatology drug development and in commercial discussions of skin-cancer immunotherapy and topical agents.

snappy: A protein that helps cells avoid death — when it's overactive, cancer cells refuse to die, which is how many blood cancers stay alive.
detail: BCL-2 was the first cancer gene discovered to work by blocking apoptosis (programmed cell death) rather than by driving growth. It's overexpressed in CLL, follicular lymphoma, and AML, and Venclexta (venetoclax) is a BCL-2 inhibitor that has reshaped treatment in those settings. You'll hear BCL-2 at hematology conferences (ASH especially) and in any pipeline discussion involving "apoptosis-targeted" oncology.

snappy: A fused gene that drives chronic myeloid leukemia — and the target of one of the first and most successful precision-medicine drugs ever made.
detail: When parts of chromosomes 9 and 22 swap places, they form the abnormal BCR-ABL gene that produces a constantly active growth signal in white blood cells, causing CML. The story is legendary in oncology: Gleevec (imatinib), launched in 2001, transformed CML from a fatal disease into a manageable chronic condition. You'll hear BCR-ABL in any drug-development pitch invoking targeted therapy as a paradigm.

snappy: How much of a drug actually makes it into your bloodstream after you take it — the rest is broken down or never absorbed.
detail: When you swallow a pill, only a fraction reaches the circulation; the rest is destroyed by stomach acid, liver enzymes, or never absorbed by the gut. IV drugs are 100% bioavailable by definition; pills can range from 5% to 95% depending on chemistry. You'll hear the term in early drug development, formulation discussions, and at FDA reviews of generic-drug equivalence.

snappy: A measurable biological signal — like a fingerprint your body leaves behind — that tells doctors what's happening inside you.
detail: Biomarkers can be molecules in your blood (such as PSA for prostate cancer), genetic mutations (such as HER2 in breast cancer), or imaging signals. Pharma uses them to diagnose disease, predict which patients will respond to a drug, and track whether a treatment is working — they're the foundation of "precision medicine."

snappy: Taking a small piece of tissue from your body to examine under a microscope — usually to confirm whether something is cancer.
detail: Biopsies range from a needle aspirate (a few cells from a lymph node) to a surgical excision (a whole lump removed). The sample is processed by a pathologist who diagnoses the disease and increasingly identifies genetic mutations that guide treatment selection. You'll hear biopsy in cancer trial design ("tumor biopsy required"), liquid-biopsy startup discussions, and any commercial conversation about companion diagnostics.

snappy: A near-identical copy of an existing biologic drug — like a generic, but more complicated to make because biologics are produced by living cells.
detail: Unlike small-molecule generics, biosimilars can't be exactly identical to their reference biologic — biology doesn't allow it — so the FDA approves them based on showing no clinically meaningful differences. They drive down prices when biologics like Remicade (infliximab) and Humira (adalimumab) lose patent protection. You'll hear biosimilar in payer discussions about cost savings and at investor briefings on biologic patent cliffs.

snappy: Cancer that starts in the lining of the bladder — most often in long-time smokers — and frequently recurs even after early-stage treatment.
detail: Bladder cancer is overwhelmingly urothelial carcinoma, with FGFR3 and TP53 mutations driving subsets. Treatment ranges from intravesical BCG for early-stage disease to systemic therapy for advanced cases — Keytruda (pembrolizumab), Padcev (enfortumab vedotin), and the FGFR inhibitor Balversa (erdafitinib) are recent additions. You'll hear bladder cancer at ASCO in commercial discussions of immunotherapy and ADC market expansion.

snappy: Hiding from patients, doctors, or both whether a person is getting the real drug or a placebo — so expectations don't bias the results.
detail: Trials can be single-blind (only patients don't know) or double-blind (neither patients nor doctors know); placebo bias is real and measurable, so blinding is essential to clean results. The unblinded readout is what triggers stock-moving headlines. You'll hear blinding in trial-design discussions, FDA review documents, and at investor conferences when companies preview Phase 3 data they expect to release.

snappy: The body's bouncer for the brain — a tight wall of cells that keeps most chemicals (including most drugs) from crossing from the blood to the brain.
detail: The BBB protects the brain from toxins but also keeps out most therapeutic drugs, making CNS conditions especially hard to treat. Crossing the BBB is a central engineering problem in neurology drug development — solved with small-molecule design, antibody shuttles, or ultrasound. You'll hear BBB in pipeline discussions for Alzheimer's, brain cancer, and any neuro-immunotherapy conversation, often as the reason something failed.

snappy: A gene that, when mutated, drives uncontrolled cell growth — found in about half of all melanomas, plus some lung and colon cancers.
detail: The BRAF V600E mutation makes the protein constantly "on," pushing cells to proliferate. Tafinlar (dabrafenib) plus Mekinist (trametinib) is the standard combo for BRAF-mutant melanoma — together they shrink tumors dramatically, though resistance often develops. You'll hear BRAF in melanoma trial readouts, precision-oncology discussions, and in any company commentary on tumor-agnostic mutation testing.

snappy: Cancer that has spread from somewhere else in the body — usually lung, breast, or melanoma — into the brain.
detail: Once cancer reaches the brain, treatment options narrow because most drugs can't cross the blood-brain barrier; whole-brain radiation, stereotactic radiosurgery, and a small group of brain-penetrant drugs (Tagrisso, Alecensa, Tukysa) become the toolkit. You'll hear brain metastases in oncology trial-enrollment criteria (often excluded) and in any drug pitch claiming "CNS activity" — a key commercial differentiator.

snappy: Two genes that normally help cells repair damaged DNA — when inherited as broken copies, they sharply raise the lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer.
detail: BRCA mutations affect roughly 1 in 400 people; carriers face up to 70% lifetime breast cancer risk and elevated risk for ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic cancers. The genes are also a treatment target — PARP inhibitors like Lynparza (olaparib) work especially well on BRCA-mutated tumors. You'll hear BRCA in genetic-testing conversations, oncology trial enrollment criteria, and at any company developing PARP-class therapies.

snappy: The most common cancer in women — classified by which receptors are present (estrogen, progesterone, HER2), because the receptors decide treatment.
detail: Breast cancer subtypes — ER+, PR+, HER2+, triple-negative — drive entirely different treatment paths: hormone therapies for ER+, HER2-targeted drugs like Herceptin (trastuzumab) and ADCs like Enhertu for HER2+, and immunotherapy plus chemo for triple-negative. You'll hear breast cancer almost everywhere in oncology — at every major conference, in every pharma pipeline, and in any commercial discussion of women's-health markets.

snappy: A signaling protein that helps cancerous B-cells survive and multiply — and the target of a major class of leukemia and lymphoma drugs.
detail: BTK is essential for B-cell development; in CLL and certain lymphomas, blocking it stops the cancer's growth signals. Imbruvica (ibrutinib) and Calquence (acalabrutinib) are the leading BTK inhibitors and major commercial products. You'll hear BTK at ASH, in CLL trial readouts, and in pipeline discussion of next-generation "covalent" or "non-covalent" inhibitors and their resistance profiles.

snappy: A very fast-growing blood cancer of B-cells — one of the most aggressive cancers known, but often curable with intensive chemo when caught early.
detail: Burkitt lymphoma is driven by a translocation that activates the MYC gene — the cancer cells double in less than a day, making it one of the fastest-growing tumors. Treatment is intensive, hospital-based combination chemotherapy, with cure rates exceeding 80% in younger patients. You'll hear Burkitt in hematology pipeline discussions and at ASH, often referenced as a benchmark for aggressive lymphoma biology.
Patient's T-cells engineered to attack cancer, revolutionary in blood cancers.
Cancer from epithelial cells, includes most common cancers (lung, breast, colon).
Cell cycle regulators targeted by palbociclib in ER+ breast cancer.
Test determining if a specific drug will work for a patient.
Immunotherapy drugs releasing 'brakes' on immune system to attack cancer.
Drug treatment killing fast-growing cells, affects cancer and healthy cells.
Bile duct cancer, may have IDH1/2 or FGFR2 mutations.
Slow-growing leukemia of B cells.
Caused by BCR-ABL fusion (Philadelphia chromosome), transformed by imatinib.
Research study testing new treatments in people, phases I-IV.
Information about how a drug is made and quality controlled.
Third most common cancer, may have APC, KRAS, BRAF mutations.
Gene-editing technology acting like molecular scissors to modify DNA.
Company hired to manage and conduct clinical trials.
Immune checkpoint targeted by ipilimumab, first checkpoint inhibitor.
Cell-killing, describes chemotherapy drugs.
Trials allowing patients to participate from home using technology.
Drugs that tag proteins for destruction rather than just blocking them.
Devastating childhood brain tumor, very poor prognosis.
Most common aggressive lymphoma.
Independent group reviewing trial data for safety.
System fixing DNA errors, deficiency causes MSI-H and immunotherapy response.
Gradually increasing drug dose in trials to find maximum safe dose.
When one drug affects another's effectiveness or toxicity.
How long a treatment keeps working once it starts.
Abnormal cell growth that's pre-cancerous.
Mutated in ~15% of lung cancers, targeted by erlotinib, osimertinib. T790M causes resistance.
European equivalent of FDA for drug approval.
Most common gynecologic cancer, often MSI-H or TP53 mutant.
What's measured in trials - OS (survival), PFS (progression-free), ORR (response rate).
Brain or spinal cord tumor from ependymal cells.
Hormone receptors in ~70% of breast cancers, targeted by tamoxifen.
Amplified in ~20% of breast cancers, targeted by trastuzumab.
Includes adenocarcinoma (reflux-associated) and squamous cell (smoking-associated).
Bone/soft tissue cancer in youth with EWSR1-FLI1 fusion.
Inactive ingredients in drugs helping deliver the active ingredient.
Epigenetic regulator mutated in lymphomas, targeted by tazemetostat.
Inherited APC mutation causing hundreds of colon polyps.
FDA designation speeding review of drugs for serious conditions.
US agency approving new drugs and ensuring safety/efficacy.
Family with fusions in cholangiocarcinoma, mutations in bladder cancer.
Very first clinical trial of a new drug in people.
Initial treatment for cancer, usually most effective.
Mutated in ~30% of AML, poor prognosis, targeted by midostaurin.
Second most common lymphoma, indolent with BCL2 translocation.
The specific recipe and form of a drug product.
Abnormal gene when two genes join together, like BCR-ABL.
Often diagnosed late, may have HER2 amplification.
Stomach cancer, subtypes include intestinal and diffuse (signet ring).
Sarcoma with KIT/PDGFRA mutations, responds dramatically to imatinib.
Most aggressive brain cancer, IDH wild-type has worse prognosis.
International quality standards for clinical trials.
Treatment inserting or replacing genes to treat disease.
Copy of brand-name drug with same active ingredient, costs less.
From reproductive cells, includes seminoma and non-seminomatous types.
Brain tumors from glial cells, graded 1-4.
Quality standards for drug production.
How abnormal cancer cells look - higher grade means more aggressive.
Time for half a drug to be eliminated from body.
Measure comparing event likelihood between two groups in trials.
Most common liver cancer, often in cirrhosis setting.
Squamous cancers of mouth, throat, larynx. HPV-positive have better prognosis.
Blood cancers including leukemias, lymphomas, myeloma.
Amplified in ~20% breast cancers, targeted by trastuzumab (Herceptin).
Include Lynch syndrome, BRCA1/2, Li-Fraumeni, FAP.
B-cell lymphoma with Reed-Sternberg cells, high cure rate.
ER and PR in breast cancer, determine endocrine therapy use.
Causes cervical cancer and some head/neck cancers, preventable with vaccine.
DNA repair defect from BRCA mutations, predicts PARP inhibitor response.
Low oxygen in tissues, tumors often have hypoxic areas resisting treatment.
Mutated in gliomas, AML, cholangiocarcinoma. Targeted by ivosidenib and enasidenib.
First targeted therapy, revolutionized CML treatment.
Molecules preventing immune attack on normal cells, hijacked by cancer.
Treatments harnessing immune system against cancer.
FDA application allowing human testing of new drugs.
The specific disease a drug is approved to treat.
Drug blocking a protein's function.
Injection directly into tumor for local effect.
Most common breast cancer type (~80%).
Drug delivery directly into vein.
Drugs blocking JAK enzymes, used in myeloproliferative neoplasms.
Most common mutation in polycythemia vera.
Signaling pathway important in immune responses and cancer.
Rare childhood leukemia with RAS pathway mutations.
Clinical trial at multiple sites testing same treatment.
Statistical method for survival curves in cancer studies.
Picture of all chromosomes showing cancer-related changes.
Proliferation marker - higher percentage means faster growing tumor.
Renal cell carcinoma, clear cell type often has VHL mutations.
Enzyme adding phosphate groups to proteins, many are drug targets.
Receptor mutated in GIST, targeted by imatinib.
Most commonly mutated RAS gene in cancer, G12C now targetable with sotorasib.
FDA-approved prescribing information for a drug.
Blood cancers from bone marrow, includes acute and chronic types.
Inherited TP53 mutation causing very high cancer risk.
Molecule binding to receptor, can be natural or drug.
Blood test detecting circulating tumor DNA for monitoring.
Larger initial dose to quickly reach therapeutic levels.
Most common lung cancer, may have EGFR, ALK, ROS1 mutations.
Immune filters where cancer often spreads first.
Cancer of lymph system, includes Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin types.
Inherited MMR gene mutations causing colorectal/endometrial cancer risk.
Freeze-drying process for preserving biological drugs.
Lab-made antibodies targeting specific proteins, names end in '-mab'.
Lower-intensity treatment to prevent disease return.
Aggressive B-cell lymphoma with cyclin D1 translocation.
p53 regulator amplified in sarcomas.
Pre-leukemic condition that can transform to AML.
How a drug works at molecular level.
Most common malignant childhood brain tumor.
Drugs blocking MAPK pathway, often combined with BRAF inhibitors.
Aggressive skin cancer, ~50% have BRAF mutations.
Cancer of mesothelial lining, usually from asbestos exposure.
Receptor with mutations/amplifications, MET exon 14 skipping targetable.
What a drug becomes after body processes it.
Cancer spread to distant sites, major cause of deaths.
DNA repair enzyme, methylation predicts temozolomide response in GBM.
Gut bacteria affecting drug response and immunotherapy efficacy.
DNA repair deficiency causing hypermutation, predicts immunotherapy response.
Plasma cell cancer in bone marrow.
DNA change that can drive cancer.
Master regulator amplified in many cancers, largely undruggable.
MYC family member amplified in neuroblastoma.
Head/neck cancer associated with EBV, endemic in Asia.
Organization providing cancer treatment guidelines.
US government's principal cancer research agency.
FDA submission for drug approval.
Uncontrolled cell death causing inflammation.
Treatment before main therapy (surgery) to shrink tumor.
Abnormal growth, can be benign or malignant.
From hormone-producing cells, includes carcinoid.
Childhood nerve tissue cancer, MYCN amplification indicates high risk.
Nerve damage from chemotherapy.
Low neutrophil count from chemotherapy, infection risk.
Technology for rapid DNA sequencing, enables precision medicine.
Diverse group of lymphomas.
Signaling pathway mutated in T-ALL.
~85% of lung cancers, includes adenocarcinoma and squamous types.
Gene fusions targeted by larotrectinib and entrectinib.
Anti-CD20 antibody for lymphomas and CLL.
Prescribing drug for condition different from FDA approval.
First approved PARP inhibitor for BRCA-mutated cancers.
Limited metastatic disease potentially curable with aggressive treatment.
Gene driving cancer when activated/amplified.
Doctor specializing in cancer treatment.
Medical specialty dealing with cancer.
Trial where everyone knows what treatment is given.
Drug for rare disease, gets FDA incentives.
Percentage of patients with tumor shrinkage.
How long patients live from treatment start.
Third-generation EGFR inhibitor overcoming T790M resistance.
Most common primary bone cancer in adolescents.
Includes epithelial (90%), germ cell, and stromal types.
'Guardian of genome' mutated in >50% of cancers.
Chemotherapy from yew tree, stabilizes microtubules.
DNA repair gene, mutations increase breast/pancreatic cancer risk.
Treatment to relieve symptoms rather than cure.
Very poor prognosis, >90% have KRAS mutations.
Drugs blocking DNA repair, effective in BRCA-mutated cancers.
No cancer found at surgery after neoadjuvant therapy.
Checkpoint proteins cancer uses to hide from immune system.
PD-1 inhibitor, first tissue-agnostic approval for MSI-H.
Treatment based on patient's specific tumor genetics.
Time until disease worsens.
I=safety, II=efficacy, III=comparison to standard.
BCR-ABL fusion causing CML and some ALL.
Key pathway, PIK3CA mutated in many cancers.
What body does to drug/what drug does to body.
Inactive substance used as control in trials.
Chemotherapy class (cisplatin, carboplatin) causing DNA damage.
Lab and animal testing before human trials.
Original cancer site, not metastases.
Most common male cancer, may have TMPRSS2-ERG fusion.
Degrader drug hijacking cell's disposal system.
Detailed plan for clinical trial.
Tumor suppressor in PI3K pathway, lost in many cancers.
Testing to ensure drug batches meet specifications.
Patient wellbeing measure in trials beyond just survival.
Heart rhythm measurement, some drugs prolong it dangerously.
Breast surgery removing quarter of breast.
Technique measuring gene expression or mutations.
Non-dividing cells that can survive chemotherapy.
Using high-energy rays to kill cancer cells.
Protein family in MAPK pathway, includes BRAF.
Gene family (KRAS, NRAS, HRAS) mutated in ~30% of cancers.
Tumor suppressor controlling cell cycle.
Data from actual clinical practice vs controlled trials.
Protein receiving signals, many are drug targets.
Cancer returning after treatment.
Disease not responding to treatment.
Related to FDA and drug approval process.
Decrease or disappearance of cancer signs/symptoms.
When disease stops responding to previously effective treatment.
Gene with fusions in lung/thyroid cancer, targeted by selpercatinib.
Childhood eye cancer from RB1 mutations.
Genetic material, target of some new therapies.
Gene fusion in ~1-2% of lung cancers, targeted by crizotinib.
Cancer from connective tissue (bone, muscle, fat), >70 subtypes.
Aggressive neuroendocrine lung cancer, usually from smoking.
Treatment used when first-line fails.
Most common testicular germ cell tumor, very curable.
Permanent growth arrest, cancer prevention mechanism.
Short RNAs for gene silencing.
Traditional drugs entering cells easily, most oral cancer drugs.
Current best accepted treatment for a condition.
Mutation in body cells, not inherited.
Cancer from squamous epithelium in skin, lung, head/neck, cervix.
Extent of cancer spread, determines treatment.
Replacing blood-forming cells, autologous or allogeneic.
Injection under skin, common for biologics.
Measurable outcome predicting clinical benefit.
Two defects together kill cells, basis for PARP inhibitors.
Lymphocytes that kill infected/cancer cells or coordinate immunity.
Drugs designed for specific molecular targets in cancer.
Recognizes antigens, engineered in TCR-T therapy.
Usually germ cell tumors, highly curable.
Ratio between toxic and effective doses.
Dose range where drug works without excessive toxicity.
Includes papillary (80%), follicular, medullary, anaplastic types.
Immune cells in tumors, more means better prognosis.
Drugs blocking kinases, names often end in '-tinib'.
Total mutations in tumor, high TMB predicts immunotherapy response.
Ecosystem around tumor affecting its behavior.
Lacks ER, PR, HER2, ~15% of breast cancers.
System for cancer staging.
Studies of drug's harmful effects and safety.
Most commonly mutated gene in cancer.
'Bench to bedside' - lab discoveries to clinical practice.
Chromosome pieces switching places, creates fusion genes.
First HER2-targeted antibody, transformed HER2+ breast cancer.
NTRK fusions targeted by larotrectinib.
Differences between cancer cells within same tumor.
Genes preventing uncontrolled growth (TP53, RB, PTEN).
Extremely high mutation burden from POLE/POLD1 or MMR deficiency.
One trial testing multiple targeted therapies for different mutations.
Cancer cells looking nothing like normal cells, very aggressive.
Metastatic cancer where original site cannot be found.
Tumors that can't be removed surgically.
In biotech: upstream=growing cells, downstream=purifying product.
Most common bladder cancer type.
Standards organization for drug quality.
Eye melanoma with different genetics than skin melanoma.
Prevention or treatment stimulating immune response, cancer vaccines in development.
Confirming biomarker or target works as expected.
Promotes blood vessel growth, targeted by bevacizumab.
Target of multi-kinase inhibitors like sunitinib.
BRAF V600E inhibitor for melanoma.
BCL2 inhibitor for CLL and AML.
Tumor suppressor mutated in clear cell kidney cancer.
Plant-derived chemo (vincristine, vinblastine) disrupting cell division.
Amount of virus in blood, monitored in infections.
Hedgehog pathway inhibitor for basal cell carcinoma.
How widely drug spreads through body.
Indolent lymphoma producing IgM, ~90% have MYD88 mutation.
Cancer cells preferring glycolysis even with oxygen present.
Time between stopping one treatment and starting another.
Graph showing each patient's tumor response in a trial.
Immune cells fighting infection, lowered by chemotherapy.
Calculating drug dose based on patient's body weight.
UN agency setting global health standards.
Sequencing all protein-coding genes to find mutations.
Normal, non-mutated version of a gene.
Dose range where drug works without excessive toxicity.
Gene in kidney development, marker in some leukemias.
Genetic conditions on X chromosome, affecting mainly males.
Radiation for imaging, chest X-rays routine in lung cancer.
Transplanting human cancer cells into mice for research.
Dry skin, common side effect of targeted therapies.
Dry mouth from radiation or medications.
Radiation delivered from outside the body.
Protein preventing cell death, overexpressed in cancer.
Radioactive isotope for targeted radiotherapy in liver cancer.
Used to produce biologics through genetic engineering.
UK's adverse drug reaction reporting system.
Anti-CTLA-4 checkpoint inhibitor for melanoma.
Amount of drug produced vs theoretical maximum in manufacturing.
Rare germ cell tumor producing AFP biomarker.
Age 18-39 in trials, some cancers affect this group differently.
Rare earth element used in cancer radiotherapy.
Prognostic marker in CLL, presence indicates aggressive disease.
